name: Mdina
description: The Silent City — a medieval walled town perched on a hilltop. Best visited early morning before the crowds. Stunning views across the island.

name: Dingli Cliffs
description: The highest point in Malta at 253 metres above sea level. Breathtaking clifftop walks with dramatic views over the Mediterranean. Best at sunset.

name: St. Peter's Pool
description: The bay is a common attraction among tourists looking to snorkel

name: Golden Bay
description: One of Malta's most popular sandy beaches on the northwest coast. Backed by gentle dunes with sunbeds, a café, and regular bus connections.

name: Qarabba Bay
description: Nestled on Malta’s northern coast, Qarraba Bay offers a serene and picturesque escape from the island’s busier hotspots

name: St. Georges Bay
description: Located in the heart of the entertainment mecca known as Paceville, it is the location where many tourists and students like to take a dip into our clean Maltese waters.

name: Għadira Bay
description: Located on Malta’s northern coast, Għadira Bay is a stunning beach known for its white sands and clear waters. Whether you’re seeking relaxation or water activities, this picturesque spot is perfect for anyone looking to enjoy a day in the sun along the Mediterranean

name: Pretty Bay
description: Known for its light blue and green waters, the beach is a popular spot for locals, particularly during the summer season. Despite being artificially filled with sand, the beach maintains a natural charm that draws visitors from far and wide.

name: St Thomas Bay
description: St Thomas Bay, located in Marsascala, Malta, is a stunning natural beach known for its breathtaking views, clear turquoise waters, and golden sands. The beach is nestled among high cliffs and rocky outcrops, adding to its picturesque scenery

name: Grand Master's Palace
description: The Grandmaster's Palace (Maltese: Il-Palazz tal-Granmastru), officially known as The Palace (Maltese: Il-Palazz), is a palace in Valletta, Malta. It was built between the 16th and 18th centuries as the palace of the Grand Master of the Knights Hospitaller, who ruled Malta from 1530 to 1798, and was also known as the Magisterial Palace (Maltese: Palazz Maġisterjali).

name: Fort St.Angelo
description: Strategically located on a hillock, at the end of the Birgu peninsula, the fort dominates the Grand Harbour. By 1274, the stronghold, previously known as Castrum Maris (castle-by-the-sea), consisted of two enclosures. The Sicilian overlords considered this fort as a guarantee of their interests in the Maltese Islands, and it was administered by loyal subjects under the title of Castellan.

name: Għar Dalam
description: No such animals have lived on the Maltese Islands for thousands of years. Rows of ancient animal bones, unearthed from Għar Dalam cave, are exhibited in the Joseph Baldacchino Hall, which still retains one of a handful of Victorian-style displays in Europe.

name: Hagar Qim
description: The site of Ħaġar Qim consists of a group of megalithic structures constructed in globigerina limestone, situated on the crest of a ridge. To the west, the site commands views over the open sea, while to the east it overlooks the edge of a large fertile plain. The main structure of Ħaġar Qim is unusual, with features and doorways along its external wall. The floor of the central passage is paved with stone slabs, while the floors in the chambers branching off this passage are made from beaten earth

name: Fort St.Elmo
description: Originally intended as a war machine, the fort was built in a strategic location at the tip of the Sceberras peninsula to face and hold back the wrath of the Ottoman armada. Offering unobstructed panoramic views of the harbours and the surrounding towns and villages, this key position was already recognised and exploited during the Medieval period

name: Domvs Romana
description: The Domvs Romana sheds light on the private life and habits of an ancient Roman aristocrat. Very little was recovered of the noble Roman town house (domvs) itself, but the intricate mosaics and other artefacts that survived for centuries are testimony enough to the original richness of this dwelling

name: Ta' Bistra Catacombs
description: TLocated half way between the ancient town of Melite and the once-important harbour of Salina, Ta’ Bistra Catacombs are, so far, the largest set of tombs and catacombs beyond the confines of the ancient city of the island. They were first recorded in the late 1800s but were only investigated in 1933 by Captain Charles Zammit, before part of the site was covered over by a new road leading down to Burmarrad. Further studies in 2004, 2013, and 2014, have brought the site back to life with new discoveries and hence, new interpretations
